behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is handler-to-queue interaction plus HTTP mapping (401 and no enqueue), not only a pure HMAC helper. A unit test of signature math alone can miss wiring bugs (e.g., handler still forwarding on failure). No real OS/network/subprocess is required; in-process app + queue double is sufficient. Higher levels are disqualified because behavior text does not require real external HTTP infrastructure or any AI/ML output.
behaviors.test_description: Run the HTTP handler in-process with raw-body parsing enabled and a queue spy/fake. Send a request with a validly shaped but incorrect `X-Signature-256`. Assert status 401 and assert queue write was never called.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: This is explicit cross-module ordering/state handoff (handler -> persistent queue -> HTTP response), so Unit is too low per MOCK-MISS and IDEA-PROBE. An in-process persistent queue implementation (e.g., local DB/file-backed module) can expose ordering bugs. System is rejected because behavior text does not require real network or subprocess boundaries.
behaviors.test_description: Use the real queue module against a local test persistence backend and instrument timestamps/order hooks around `enqueue` commit and response send. Send a valid webhook and assert enqueue commit completes before 200 is emitted.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: Behavior is async decoupling between request path and downstream processor. The real failure is module interaction (await vs fire-and-forget/queued), not single-function logic. No real OS/network/subprocess is necessary; a controllable delayed downstream module in-process can reveal the bug. Higher levels are disqualified by lack of real infrastructure requirement.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler + queue + downstream worker stub where processing blocks on a deferred promise. Send valid event and assert response completes before deferred is resolved; then resolve deferred and verify processing eventually runs.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: This is a wall-clock latency SLO under concurrency pressure. Real runtime scheduling, socket handling, and server behavior can create failures hidden by in-process fakes or timer mocks (REAL-INFRA, MOCK-HIDE). Integration is therefore too low. Agentic/Workflow are disqualified because no AI/ML decisions are involved.
behaviors.test_description: Start the service as a real process, drive concurrent HTTP requests, and deliberately slow downstream processing for prior events. Measure end-to-end response time for new webhook requests and assert each returns within 3 seconds.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Idempotency here is state handoff across requests and persistence boundary (dedupe key vs queue write), so Unit is too low. Use real dedupe/queue storage path in-process to catch duplicate-write bugs. System is rejected because behavior text does not require external network/subprocess effects.
behaviors.test_description: Using the real persistence-backed queue/dedupe path, send the same event ID twice. Assert first request writes once and second request does not create an additional queue record.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is queue backpressure signal propagation into HTTP semantics (status + header), a module interaction concern. Unit-only tests of header formatting can miss incorrect mapping from queue-full condition. No real OS/network/subprocess is required; in-process queue-full condition is enough. Agentic/Workflow not applicable.
behaviors.test_description: Run handler with queue module configured to return `full` on enqueue. Send valid webhook and assert HTTP 503 plus `Retry-After` header value per contract, and no downstream processing starts.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: This validates the queue component with its real persistence/config wiring, beyond pure arithmetic unit logic. A mock queue would always pass and hide real enforcement bugs (MOCK-HIDE). System is rejected because configured logical capacity enforcement does not inherently require real external OS/network/subprocess behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real persistent queue against a local test backend with small configured capacity. Enqueue up to limit, then attempt one additional event and assert rejection/error path is returned and stored count does not exceed capacity.
